title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - William W. Campbell, Ernest C. Moore, and others, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Dr. Campbell, outgoing President of the University of California (on left), Dr. Moore, Director of UCLA, and others converse on the steps of Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Dr. Sproul, incoming University of California President, stands on the steps of Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. [Note: Handwritten date in the margin is incorrect]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: George and Ira Gershwin with students lounging in the sun, 1937
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Ira Gershwin (left) and George Gershwin (right) sit on a sundeck with UCLA students at George's home. The songwriting duo had just donated their song "Strike up the band" to UCLA. The students include Kay Dodge, Vera Nell Gilmer, Catherine Cockrell, Georgette Foster, Dorothy Oswald, Grace Wolfskill, and Alice Waldron.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Physics-Biology Building (Humanities Building) south entrance detail, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: The inscription over the building's south entrance--"Nothing is too wonderful to be true"--is from a quote by Michael Faraday, English chemist and physicist. Hoover took this photograph during the ongoing construction of the Westwood campus' first academic year. The Physics-Biology Building was renamed Kinsey Hall and is today known as the Humanities Building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Kerckhoff Hall dedication - Governor James Rolph, Jr. speaking, 1931
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in "California of the Southland," page 54.
The governor was the chief guest at the dedication ceremonies for Kerckhoff Hall, the new student union for the Westwood campus.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Photographed looking east. From L to R: Royce Hall, Janss Steps, Library (Powell Library), and Education Building (Moore Hall). The Athletic Field is in the foreground.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Midway through construction, the north side of the Education Building (Moore Hall) is viewed from the southwestern corner of the Library (Powell Library).
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Commencement at the Open Air Theater, c.1941
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: UCLA commencement ceremonies were held at the campus Open Air Theater from 1941 to 1949. This photograph likely depicts the first commencement held on the Westwood campus on June 14, 1941. The Open Air Theater (sometimes referred to as the Greek Theater) was located at the south end of campus where the Public Health Building stands today. The Open Air Theater was bulldozed for the expanding campus in 1952. [Note: Original print is damaged]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Sorority member in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: An unidentified member of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority appears in period costume for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. She stands in the yard of an unidentified building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Commencement at the Open Air Theater, c.1941
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: UCLA commencement ceremonies were held at the campus Open Air Theater from 1941 to 1949. This photograph likely depicts the first commencement held on the Westwood campus on June 14, 1941. The Open Air Theater (sometimes referred to as the Greek Theater) was located at the south end of campus where the Public Health Building stands today. The Open Air Theater was bulldozed for the expanding campus in 1952.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Alumni event at Lake Arrowhead - Dinner singing, 1944
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Annual alumni events in Lake Arrowhead's North Shore Tavern (Main Lodge) were common even before the university received the facility as a donation in 1957. Known today as the UCLA Conference Center, the Lake Arrowhead facility is located about 90 miles east of Los Angeles.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Models of Bridge and first four buildings, c.1927
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in "California of the Southland," page 29.

This model of the Westwood campus' Bridge and first four buildings was prepared by A. E. Davie, Superintendent of Grounds and Buildings. From L to R: The Library (Powell Library), Physics-Biology Building (Humanities Building), Royce Hall, and Chemistry Building (Haines Hall).
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - William W. Campbell and Robert G. Sproul, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in "UCLA on the Move," page 65. Also used in "California of the Southland," page 52.

Dr. Campbell, outgoing President of the University of California, and Dr. Sproul, incoming President of the University of California, stand on the steps of Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - Earle R. Hedrick and Helen Hedrick, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Dr. Hedrick, mathematics professor and later Provost of UCLA, walks arm-in-arm with his wife on the Esplanade before Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. [Note: This image is printed in reverse.]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Registration - Sale of student ID cards at Royce Hall, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Various students or staff--including Lawrence Houston (UCLA 1930), center in dark suit--sell ASUCLA membership cards at the north (rear) entrance to Royce Hall. This rear entrance to Royce Hall is no longer visible due to the addition of a scenery shop in 1953 and further renovation in 1984 and 1998. Royce Hall was the center of activity during the mid-February registration for the "second half-year" (Feb. - June) of the first academic year on the new Westwood campus.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Registration - Mary Ellen Hohiesel and another student on the Esplanade, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in the 1930 "Southern Campus" UCLA yearbook, page 122.

Mary Ellen Hohiesel, sophomore class president, crosses the Esplanade before Royce Hall with an unidentified student. The Esplanade is more generally referred to as Royce quadrangle and is known today as Dickson Plaza. Royce Hall was the center of activity during the mid-February registration for the "second half-year" (Feb. - June) of the first academic year on the new Westwood campus.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Sorority members in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Members of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority appear in period costume--both female and male garb--for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. The photograph is taken in the yard of an unidentified building.

title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - Participants in regalia exiting Royce Hall, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: From L to R: William W. Campbell, outgoing President of the University of California, William H. Crocker, Chairman of the Board of Regents, and George E. Vincent, former President of the Rockefeller Institute. Over Dr. Vincent's shoulder is Lieutenant Governor Herschel L. Carnahan, who officially accepted the new campus buildings on behalf of Governor C. C. Young. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. [Note: Handwritten date in the margin is incorrect]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

description_tesim: A man identified by Hoover as "Ed Dickinson" (on left) walks with Virgil "Wes" Cazel, junior class President, across the Esplanade before Royce Hall. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. [Note: Handwritten date in the margin is incorrect]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Aerial view of UCLA and Westwood Hills, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Distant aerial view of campus and Westwood Hills looking southeast from an unidentified aircraft. Photographed during the first academic year on the Westwood campus, only six structures were present: The Education Building (Moore Hall), Royce Hall, the Library (College Library, subsequently Powell Library), the Chemistry Building (Haines Hall), the Physics-Biology Building (Kinsey Hall, and subsequently the Humanities Building), and the Bridge. Construction has begun on Kerckhoff Hall and temporary buildings are seen in the foreground.

title_tesim: Founders Day celebration at the Clark Memorial Library, 1948
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Celebrants include (from L to R): Lawrence C. Powell (University Librarian), Paul A. Dodd (Dean of the College of Letters and Science), Vern O. title_tesim: Vermont Avenue campus - Student union rally on Main Quad, 1929
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: A student union rally on the Main Quad before Millspaugh Hall during the final semester on the Vermont Avenue campus. The Los Angeles State Normal School moved to this twenty-five acre site on North Vermont Avenue near downtown Los Angeles in September 1914, where it became the Southern Branch of the University of California in July 1919. After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. title_tesim: Sorority members in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Louise E. Brown (Hoover)--on the left--and another member of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority appear in period costume--both female and male garb--for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. The photograph is taken in the yard of an unidentified building.

title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - William W. Campbell and Ernest C. Moore, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in "UCLA on the Move," page 49.

Dr. Campbell, outgoing President of the University of California (on left), and Dr. Moore, Director of UCLA, converse on the steps of Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Vermont Avenue campus - Ernest C. Moore before Millspaugh Hall, c.1928
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Ernest C. Moore (Director of UCLA) walks toward the entrance to the Vermont Avenue campus with Millspaugh Hall (the administrative building) in the background. The Los Angeles State Normal School moved to this twenty-five acre site on North Vermont Avenue near downtown Los Angeles in September 1914, where it became the Southern Branch of the University of California in July 1919. After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.
Photographed used in "UCLA on the Move", page 14.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Sorority members in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Members of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority--including Louise E. Brown (Hoover) second from left--appear in men's and women's period costume for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. The photograph is taken in the yard of an unidentified building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

From L to R, the buildings include Royce Hall, the Library (Powell Library), and the Education Building (Moore Hall). The first permanent buildings of the elementary school--today known as the UCLA Lab School--appeared on this site in 1950.
Photograph used in "California of the Southland," page 63.

The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. The photograph is taken in the yard of an unidentified building.

title_tesim: Stand collapse aftermath at Tournament of Roses parade, 1926
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Rescue workers and official examine the aftermath of a stand collapse during the Tournament of Roses Festival parade on New Year's Day 1926 in Pasadena, California. The fatal collapse sent hundreds of parade watchers to the hospital and left at least four people dead. Hoover, who at the time was attending Pasadena Junior College, took this photograph of the recovery effort as curious onlookers were kept back by rows of men with outstretched arms. Hoover would go on to attend UCLA, where he became the official photographer for several publications and an  unofficial chronicler of UCLA events.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Vermont Avenue campus - Millspaugh Hall with eucalyptus trees, 1928
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Viewed here through a line of eucalyptus trees, Millspaugh Hall was the administration building of the Vermont Avenue campus. The Los Angeles State Normal School moved to this twenty-five acre site on North Vermont Avenue near downtown Los Angeles in September 1914, where it became the Southern Branch of the University of California in July 1919. After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.
Photograph used in "California of the Southland," page 19.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.

title_tesim: Stand collapse victim on stretcher at Tournament of Roses parade, 1926
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Rescue workers and volunteers lift an injured person on a stretcher into an automobile following a stand collapse during the Tournament of Roses Festival parade on New Year's Day 1926 in Pasadena, California. The fatal collapse sent hundreds of parade watchers to the hospital and left at least four people dead. Hoover, who at the time was attending Pasadena Junior College, would go on to attend UCLA, where he became the official photographer for several publications and an unofficial chronicler of UCLA events.

The Associated Students UCLA comprise the elected student government on campus. Piper and Keith both graduated in 1930, whereas Stanley had graduated in 1928 and served his position as an alum. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28.

title_tesim: Dedication ceremony - Joe Crail and Clifford Barrett, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: A man identified by Hoover as Joe Crail stands with Dr. Barrett, philosophy professor, at the entrance to Royce Hall following the Dedication ceremony. Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28. [Note: Handwritten date in the margin is incorrect]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Sorority members in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Members of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority--including Louise E. Brown (Hoover) second from left--appear in men's and women's period costume for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. The photograph is taken in the yard of an unidentified building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

description_tesim: The Physics-Biology Building as photographed from the Goodyear Blimp looking northwest. The Library (College Library, subsequently Powell Library) appears in the background to the left and Royce Hall is on the right. The Physics-Biology Building was renamed Kinsey Hall and is today known as the Humanities Building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Vermont Avenue campus - Alumni Spring Banquet, 1928
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: An exterior, nighttime photograph of the Alumni Spring Banquet, an annual tradition seen here before Millspaugh Hall on the Main Quad of the Vermont Avenue campus. The Los Angeles State Normal School moved to this twenty-five acre site on North Vermont Avenue near downtown Los Angeles in September 1914, where it became the Southern Branch of the University of California in July 1919. After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.
Photograph used in "California of the Southland", page 80 [with the date identified as 1929].
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)

Known formally as the "Dedication of the New Campus and New Buildings of the University of California at Los Angeles", the event brought together regents, professors, students, alumni, and representatives of other educational institutions for four sessions held on March 27 and 28.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Registration - Students lined up on the Esplanade facing Royce Hall, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Students lined up on the Esplanade on their way into Royce Hall (not pictured). The Physics Biology Building (Humanities Building)--on left--and the Library (Powell Library)--on right--appear in the background. The Esplanade is more generally called Royce quadrangle and is known today as Dickson Plaza. Royce Hall was the center of activity during the mid-February registration for the "second half-year" (Feb. - June) of the first academic year on the new Westwood campus.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Aerial view of UCLA looking west to Santa Monica, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Distant aerial view of campus looking west from an unidentified aircraft. Photographed during the first academic year on the Westwood campus, only six structures were present: The Education Building (Moore Hall), Royce Hall, the Library (College Library, subsequently Powell Library), the Chemistry Building (Haines Hall), the Physics-Biology Building (Kinsey Hall, and subsequently the Humanities Building), and the Bridge.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Alumi Association Annual Picnic group photo with coaches, 1952
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Fathers and sons pose with UCLA coaches in this photo during the annual alumni picnic at UCLA's Spaulding Field, which opened Homecoming week festivities with food, fun, games, and contests. Included: William C. Ackerman (ASUCLA Executive Director and former men's tennis coach; front row, third from left), Red Sanders (football coach; front row, seventh from left), J.D. Morgan (men's tennis coach; fourth row, fifth from left), John B. Jackson (UCLA Alumni Association Executive Secretary; fifth row, second from left), and John Wooden (men's basketball coach; fifth row, fourth from left).
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: UCLA commencement ceremonies were held at the campus Open Air Theater from 1941 to 1949. This photograph likely depicts the first commencement held on the Westwood campus on June 14, 1941. The Open Air Theater (sometimes referred to as the Greek Theater) was located at the south end of campus where the Public Health Building stands today. The Open Air Theater was bulldozed for the expanding campus in 1952.

description_tesim: Studio portrait of Thelner B. Hoover (UCLA 1930), professional photographer in Los Angeles, Califronia. While at UCLA, Hoover was the official photographer for several UCLA publications and an unofficial chronicler of numerous campus events. He became the manager of the Westwood Village Studio--a photography studio--in 1938.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: UCLA notables gather for groundbreaking ceremony on the new Westwood campus. Elizabeth Janss is seen in the foreground being helped onto the bulldozer.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Aerial view of Westwood Village and UCLA, 1938
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Distant aerial view of Westwood Village and campus as photographed from an unidentified aircraft looking north.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Sorority member (Louise E. Brown) in costume for skit, c.1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Louise E. Brown (Hoover) of the Delta Zeta, Alpha Chi chapter sorority appears in period costume for a skit titled "All that glitters". The theme of the skit likely references the pioneering spirit of the class of 1929-30 (the first class to meet on the still-rugged Westwood campus; the class (the '29ers) often compared itself to the gold rush '49ers. The skit may have been for a sorority gala or a campus event, such as Campus Capers or the all-female Hi-Jinx. Brown (UCLA 1930) stands in the yard of an unidentified building.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: George Gershwin and students singing at the piano, 1937
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Photograph used in "UCLA on the Move", page 155.

Gershwin and UCLA students sing "Strike up the band" at Gershwin's home. The composer had donated the song to UCLA in 1937. The students are (from L to R) Kay Dodge, Vera Nell Gilmer (seated), Catherine Cockrell, Georgette Foster, Dorothy Oswald, Grace Wolfskill, and Alice Waldron.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: UCLA commencement ceremonies were held at the campus Open Air Theater from 1941 to 1949. This photograph likely depicts the first commencement held on the Westwood campus on June 14, 1941. The Open Air Theater (sometimes referred to as the Greek Theater) was located at the south end of campus where the Public Health Building stands today. The Open Air Theater was bulldozed for the expanding campus in 1952.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Gold Shield members pose in front of the University Residence. From L to R: Carroll Johnson, Louise E. Brown Hoover (UCLA 1930), Dorothy Sheard Allen, and [Ella Mae Manwarring]. Formed in 1936, the Gold Shield is a women's alumni group that hosts UCLA events, sponsors lectures, and raises scholarship funds. Louise Hoover, the photographer's wife, was an active member of Gold Shield for 33 years.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: The south (or front) side of Josiah Royce Hall appears here midway through its construction. The perspective is from the Library (Powell Library), which was at a similar stage of construction at the time.
Photograph used in "UCLA on the Move," page 61. Also used in "California of the Southland," page 38.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

description_tesim: Annual alumni events in Lake Arrowhead's North Shore Tavern (Main Lodge) were common even before the university received the facility as a donation in 1957. Known today as the UCLA Conference Center, the Lake Arrowhead facility is located about 90 miles east of Los Angeles.

subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Two male students dressed as women attempt to gate-crash this annual women-only variety show put on by the Associated Women Students and are escorted by female students dressed as police out the doors of Royce Hall.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Exterior shot of three men standing next to tail section of Lockheed Vega airplane. From L to R: Ray K. Candee (Manager of the 1929 "Southern Campus" UCLA yearbook) (UCLA 1929), Lieutenant Harold Bromley (pilot), and  John Brewer Avery (Editor of the UCLA "Southern Campus" 1929 yearbook). Avery and Candee accompanied Hoover, the photographer, on flights for aerial photography of the Westwood campus and surrounding areas.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Library (Powell Library) roof view looking east, 1928
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Shortly after the Library's completion, Hoover took this roof-level photograph of the southern octagonal dome and cupola looking east. The Physics Biology Building (Humanities Building) appears in the background. Originally known simply as the Library, Powell Library was known as College Library prior to 1966.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

title_tesim: Moving to Westwood campus - Unloading into Royce, 1929
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Photograph used in "California of the Southland", page 48.

May 31, 1929, a day that came to be known as "Moving Day,"  saw a caravan of more than 500 vehicles filled with students, moving boxes, furniture, and equipment make its way from Vermont Avenue to the new Westwood campus. Ernest C. Moore, Director of UCLA, declared the day a half-holiday, and the ROTC band accompanied the procession. In this photograph, students unload a truck before Royce Hall. [Note: Handwritten date in the margin is incorrect]
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Snowfall on campus - Royce Hall and Esplanade, 1932
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: Photograph used in "UCLA on the Move," page 76. Also used in "California of the Southland," page 56.

A rare snowfall covers the Esplanade and Royce Hall, viewed here from the foot of the Physics Biology Building (Humanities Building). The Esplanade is more generally called Royce quadrangle and is known today as Dickson Plaza. The edge of the Library (Powell Library) is visible on the left. Hoover took this photograph in the early morning before the pristine snowfall was disturbed by excited students.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Aerial view of Janss Steps and campus, 1930
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
subject_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall
description_tesim: A distant view of campus from the Goodyear Blimp looking southeast. From L to R: The Chemistry Building (Haines Hall), Royce Hall, Physics-Biology Building (Kinsey Hall, and subsequently Humanities Building), Library (College Library, subsequently Powell Library), and Education Building (Moore Hall). Janss Steps and temporary buildings are in the  foreground.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection
combined_subject_ssim: University of California, Los Angeles--Royce Hall

title_tesim: Vermont Avenue campus - Push ball contest, c.1928
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)
description_tesim: Female students engage in a push ball contest on the Vermont Avenue campus. The Los Angeles State Normal School moved to this twenty-five acre site on North Vermont Avenue near downtown Los Angeles in September 1914, where it became the Southern Branch of the University of California in July 1919. After a name change to the University of California at Los Angeles (UCLA) in 1927, the university moved to its present location in Westwood in the May 1929.
member_of_collections_ssim: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Thelner and Louise Hoover Photographic Collection (Univ. Archives Rec. Ser. 647)

description_tesim: May 31, 1929, a day that came to be known as "Moving Day,"  saw a caravan of more than 500 vehicles filled with students, moving boxes, furniture, and equipment make its way from Vermont Avenue to the new Westwood campus. title_tesim: Guards disputing the passage of William Sachtleben and Thomas Allen to the Acropolis on bicycle, Athens, 1891
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative: 21 / 14
Text from negative sleeve: No. 12. March 13, 1891. The guards disputing our passage through the Propylaea up to the Acropolis.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Sachtleben (William Lewis) Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph taken during William Lewis Sachtleben's stay in Athens before embarking on a bicycle journey across Asia with Thomas Gaskell Allen Jr.

View of William Sachtleben with his Humber bicycle (L) and Thomas Allen with his bicycle,  with Acropolis guards and others at the top of the steps of the Propylaea. Sachtleben and Allen are trying to gain access to the Acropolis. The Parthenon is visible in the background. The guard in the foreground is wearing traditional Greek dress including a white pleated foustanella, a white shirt with wide sleeves, and a fermeli (vest).

Sachtleben's 1891 photographs of his bicycle tour across Asia have a circular format.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: No. 5. Jan. 28, 1891. Another statue in the Stoa of the Giants. One of the so-called Atlantis.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Sachtleben (William Lewis) Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph taken during William Lewis Sachtleben's stay in Athens before embarking on a bicycle journey across Asia with Thomas Gaskell Allen Jr.

Photograph of William Lewis Sachtleben at the Stoa of the Giants in the Agora in Athens. Sachtleben stands with his bicycle next to a colossal statue on a pedestal. Another unidentified man stands on the left. A high stone wall and two houses are visible in the background.

The center of the Agora was open until a theater was build there, called the Odeion of Agrippa. It was destroyed by fire in A.D. 267 and in about A.D. 400 the Stoa of the Giants (also known at the Palace of the Giants or the Gymnasium) was built using the colossal Giant and Triton statues from the debris of the Odeion of Agrippa on its north side.
member_of_collections_ssim: Sachtleben (William Lewis) Papers. Collection 1841

title_tesim: Gateway entering Qazvin from the west, Qazvīn, Iran, 1891
caption_tesim: Handwritten on negative: 4498 / 55

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Sachtleben (William Lewis) Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: During the 9th century A.D. seven gates made entrance to the city possible. In Qajar period there existed nine gates surrounding the city which were connected to each other through a wall around the city. These gates (darvāzeh in Persian) were: Panbeh Riseh,  Sheikh Abad, Rasht, Maghlävak, Khandaghbar, Shahzadeh Hossein, Mossala, Tehran, Räh e Koushk. Due to 20th century urban expansion, unfortunately only the last two gates remain standing.
Photograph taken during William Lewis Sachtleben's bicycle journey across Asia with Thomas Gaskell Allen Jr.
View of the gateway entering Qazvin from the west.
